metadata.data_vintage_note: Florida SB 1614 (March 2023) created a 60-day public-access privacy window. Post-2022 non-fatal crashes are not publicly available without a Signal4 authenticated account. We use NHTSA FARS for 2023-2024 fatal data so the highest-severity tier stays current.

properties.name: Cervantes Street (W) — Pace to A
properties.summary: Wide road where people cross to reach buses, jobs, and clinics. Crashes here often involve people walking.
properties.message: Cervantes is the single highest-risk walking corridor downtown. Shorter, protected crossings would help transit riders and neighbors most. Cervantes is a primary walking route to downtown jobs, clinics, and transit. Shorter, signalized crossings are a proven way to reduce pedestrian conflicts here.

psc.title: Corridor Access Management
psc.tagline: Every intersection, from a signalized intersection to an unpaved driveway, has the potential for conflicts between vehicles, pedestrians, and bicyclists.
psc.narrative: Access management refers to the design, application, and control of entry and exit points along a roadway, including intersections and driveways. By managing the number and types of conflict points—locations where the travel paths of two users intersect—this countermeasure enhances safety and reduces total and fatal/injury crashes for all modes.
psc.crash_reduction: 5-23% reduction in total crashes along 2-lane rural roads and 25-31% reduction in fatal and injury crashes along urban/suburban arterials.
psc.source_full: Federal Highway Administration — Proven Safety Countermeasure
psc.image_alt: Illustration: This schematic shows a signalized intersection with adjacent corridor access points. The main road (labeled "MAINLINE") has multiple access points on both sides, such as driveways or minor street connections. There is a signalized intersection where the mainline intersects with a "CROSS STREET." The illustration highlights specific areas of the intersection including the "Mainline approach corner," "Mainline receiving corner," "Corner clearance," and several "Access points." Source: FHWA
psc.why_text: intersection crashes (261 total, 17 KSI) make this corridor a strong fit.

psc.title: Pedestrian Safety Islands
psc.tagline: A pedestrian safety island reduces the exposure time experienced by a pedestrian in the intersection.
psc.narrative: Pedestrian safety islands reduce exposure time in intersections by allowing pedestrians to cross one direction of traffic at a time. They are particularly effective on wide streets with high speeds or volumes, and should be at least 6 feet wide to accommodate bicycles or strollers. Design elements often include a 'nose' that extends past the crosswalk to protect waiting pedestrians and slow turning vehicles.
psc.source_full: National Association of City Transportation Officials
psc.image_alt: Pedestrians crossing at a safety island in Chicago, IL
psc.why_text: intersection crashes (261 total, 17 KSI) make this corridor a strong fit.

psc.title: Transit Streets
psc.tagline: Building streets to support transit entails considering every passenger’s trip from start to finish, from the walking environment to dedicated transit lanes.
psc.narrative: Transit streets prioritize transit over general traffic, providing safe places for people to walk and access stops. Dedicated transit lanes and operational improvements like signal timings minimize delay at intersections and ensure transit moves freely regardless of congestion. These design elements aim to create a passenger experience competitive with driving while enhancing the public realm.
psc.source_full: National Association of City Transportation Officials
psc.image_alt: A green and white streetcar in a city environment with buildings and pedestrians.
psc.why_text: intersection crashes (261 total, 17 KSI) make this corridor a strong fit.

Capital costs only. Not an engineering BCA.

hotspot.method: Corridor divided into fixed, non-overlapping 0.5-mile windows; crashes assigned by nearest-point projection onto the centerline. psc.title: Dedicated Left- and Right-Turn Lanes at Intersections
psc.tagline: Auxiliary turn lanes provide physical separation between turning traffic and through traffic at intersections to reduce the potential for crashes.
psc.narrative: Auxiliary turn lanes provide physical separation between turning traffic that is slowing or stopped and adjacent through traffic at approaches to intersections . They provide for deceleration prior to a turn and storage for vehicles waiting to complete a turn, reducing the potential for crashes . This countermeasure primarily reduces collisions of vehicles turning left across opposing through traffic and rear-end collisions with vehicles following closely behind .
psc.crash_reduction: 28-48% reduction in total crashes
psc.source_full: Federal Highway Administration — Proven Safety Countermeasure
psc.image_alt: Left- and right-turn lanes at a two-way stop-controlled intersection.
psc.why_text: intersection crashes (267 total, 24 KSI) make this corridor a strong fit.

psc.title: Systemic Application of Multiple Low-Cost Countermeasures at Stop-Controlled Intersections
psc.tagline: This systemic approach to intersection safety increases driver awareness and recognition of intersections and potential conflicts.
psc.narrative: This systemic approach to intersection safety involves deploying a package of multiple low-cost countermeasures, including enhanced signing and pavement markings, at a large number of stop-controlled intersections within a jurisdiction. These countermeasures increase driver awareness and recognition of the intersections and potential conflicts, thereby reducing fatal and injury crashes.
psc.crash_reduction: 10% reduction of fatal and injury crashes at all locations/types/areas
psc.source_full: Federal Highway Administration — Proven Safety Countermeasure
psc.image_alt: Example of countermeasures on the through approach. Source: South Carolina DOT

psc.title: Leading Pedestrian Interval
psc.tagline: LPIs reduce potential conflicts between pedestrians and turning vehicles.
psc.narrative: A leading pedestrian interval (LPI) gives pedestrians the opportunity to enter the crosswalk at an intersection 3-7 seconds before vehicles are given a green indication. This allows pedestrians to better establish their presence in the crosswalk before vehicles have priority to turn right or left. By increasing visibility and reducing conflicts, this countermeasure can lead to a 13% reduction in pedestrian-vehicle crashes at intersections.
psc.crash_reduction: 13% reduction in pedestrian-vehicle crashes at intersections
psc.source_full: Federal Highway Administration — Proven Safety Countermeasure

psc.title: Roundabouts
psc.tagline: The modern roundabout is an intersection with a circular configuration that safely and efficiently moves traffic and substantially reduces fatal and injury crashes.
psc.narrative: Roundabouts feature channelized, curved approaches that reduce vehicle speed, entry yield control that gives right-of-way to circulating traffic, and counterclockwise flow around a central island that minimizes conflict points. The net result of lower speeds and reduced conflicts is an environment where crashes that cause injury or fatality are substantially reduced.
psc.crash_reduction: 82% reduction in fatal and injury crashes
psc.source_full: Federal Highway Administration — Proven Safety Countermeasure
psc.image_alt: Example of a single-lane roundabout.
psc.why_text: intersection crashes (29 total, 2 KSI) make this corridor a strong fit.

psc.title: Road Safety Audit
psc.tagline: Road Safety Audits (RSAs) provide an independent, multidisciplinary assessment of roadway safety to address limitations in traditional safety review procedures and reduce crashes.
psc.narrative: RSAs are performed by a multidisciplinary team independent of the project to consider all road users and account for human factors. They are documented in a formal report and require a formal response from the road owner, helping to reduce the number and severity of crashes due to safer designs.
psc.crash_reduction: 10-60% reduction in total crashes
psc.source_full: Federal Highway Administration — Proven Safety Countermeasure
psc.image_alt: Multi-disciplinary team performs field review during an RSA.

psc.title: Backplates with Retroreflective Borders
psc.tagline: Backplates with retroreflective borders improve the visibility of traffic signals to reduce crashes at signalized intersections.
psc.narrative: Backplates added to a traffic signal head improve the visibility of the illuminated face of the signal by introducing a controlled-contrast background. The improved visibility is made even more conspicuous by framing it with a 1- to 3-inch yellow retroreflective border, which is highly visible in both daytime and nighttime conditions. This treatment acts as a human factors enhancement that reduces total crashes at signalized intersections.
psc.crash_reduction: 15% reduction in total crashes
psc.source_full: Federal Highway Administration — Proven Safety Countermeasure
psc.image_alt: A conventional traffic signal with a signal backplate and yellow retroreflective border.

psc.title: Pedestrian Safety Islands
psc.tagline: A pedestrian safety island reduces the exposure time experienced by a pedestrian in the intersection.
psc.narrative: Pedestrian safety islands reduce exposure time in intersections by allowing pedestrians to cross one direction of traffic at a time. They are particularly effective on wide streets with high speeds or volumes, and should be at least 6 feet wide to accommodate bicycles or strollers. Design elements often include a 'nose' that extends past the crosswalk to protect waiting pedestrians and slow turning vehicles.
psc.source_full: National Association of City Transportation Officials
psc.image_alt: Pedestrians crossing at a safety island in Chicago, IL
psc.why_text: intersection crashes (73 total, 3 KSI) make this corridor a strong fit.

psc.title: Transit Streets
psc.tagline: Building streets to support transit entails considering every passenger’s trip from start to finish, from the walking environment to dedicated transit lanes.
psc.narrative: Transit streets prioritize transit over general traffic, providing safe places for people to walk and access stops. Dedicated transit lanes and operational improvements like signal timings minimize delay at intersections and ensure transit moves freely regardless of congestion. These design elements aim to create a passenger experience competitive with driving while enhancing the public realm.
psc.source_full: National Association of City Transportation Officials
psc.image_alt: A green and white streetcar in a city environment with buildings and pedestrians.

properties.summary: Busy shopping area with long wait times to cross.

psc.title: Leading Pedestrian Interval
psc.tagline: LPIs reduce potential conflicts between pedestrians and turning vehicles.
psc.narrative: A leading pedestrian interval (LPI) gives pedestrians the opportunity to enter the crosswalk at an intersection 3-7 seconds before vehicles are given a green indication. This allows pedestrians to better establish their presence in the crosswalk before vehicles have priority to turn right or left. By increasing visibility and reducing conflicts, this countermeasure can lead to a 13% reduction in pedestrian-vehicle crashes at intersections.
psc.crash_reduction: 13% reduction in pedestrian-vehicle crashes at intersections
psc.source_full: Federal Highway Administration — Proven Safety Countermeasure

psc.title: Dedicated Left- and Right-Turn Lanes at Intersections
psc.tagline: Auxiliary turn lanes provide physical separation between turning traffic and through traffic at intersections to reduce the potential for crashes.
psc.narrative: Auxiliary turn lanes provide physical separation between turning traffic that is slowing or stopped and adjacent through traffic at approaches to intersections . They provide for deceleration prior to a turn and storage for vehicles waiting to complete a turn, reducing the potential for crashes . This countermeasure primarily reduces collisions of vehicles turning left across opposing through traffic and rear-end collisions with vehicles following closely behind .
psc.crash_reduction: 28-48% reduction in total crashes
psc.source_full: Federal Highway Administration — Proven Safety Countermeasure
psc.image_alt: Left- and right-turn lanes at a two-way stop-controlled intersection.

psc.title: Systemic Application of Multiple Low-Cost Countermeasures at Stop-Controlled Intersections
psc.tagline: This systemic approach to intersection safety increases driver awareness and recognition of intersections and potential conflicts.
psc.narrative: This systemic approach to intersection safety involves deploying a package of multiple low-cost countermeasures, including enhanced signing and pavement markings, at a large number of stop-controlled intersections within a jurisdiction. These countermeasures increase driver awareness and recognition of the intersections and potential conflicts, thereby reducing fatal and injury crashes.
psc.crash_reduction: 10% reduction of fatal and injury crashes at all locations/types/areas
psc.source_full: Federal Highway Administration — Proven Safety Countermeasure
psc.image_alt: Example of countermeasures on the through approach. Source: South Carolina DOT
psc.why_text: intersection crashes (592 total, 42 KSI) make this corridor a strong fit.

psc.title: Corridor Access Management
psc.tagline: Every intersection, from a signalized intersection to an unpaved driveway, has the potential for conflicts between vehicles, pedestrians, and bicyclists.
psc.narrative: Access management refers to the design, application, and control of entry and exit points along a roadway, including intersections and driveways. By managing the number and types of conflict points—locations where the travel paths of two users intersect—this countermeasure enhances safety and reduces total and fatal/injury crashes for all modes.
psc.crash_reduction: 5-23% reduction in total crashes along 2-lane rural roads and 25-31% reduction in fatal and injury crashes along urban/suburban arterials.
psc.source_full: Federal Highway Administration — Proven Safety Countermeasure

properties.summary: Drivers speed on a long, straight, scenic road shared with cyclists.
properties.message: Showing drivers their speed lowers it, and a bike lane gives riders a clear space. Scenic Highway sees frequent lane-departure crashes. Rumble strips and speed feedback are among the cheapest interventions in the FHWA catalog.

psc.title: Longitudinal Rumble Strips and Stripes on Two-Lane Roads
psc.tagline: Rumble strips and stripes are designed to address roadway departure crashes by alerting distracted, drowsy, or otherwise inattentive drivers who drift from their lane.
psc.narrative: Longitudinal rumble strips are milled or raised elements intended to alert drivers through vibration and sound that their vehicle has left the travel lane. Designed to address roadway departure crashes, they alert distracted, drowsy, or inattentive drivers who drift from their lane. These countermeasures are most effective when deployed systemically on two-lane roads.
psc.crash_reduction: 44-64% reduction in head-on fatal and injury crashes on two-lane rural roads.
psc.source_full: Federal Highway Administration — Proven Safety Countermeasure
psc.image_alt: Shoulder rumble strips and center line rumble stripes are installed on this roadway. Source: FHWA
psc.why_text: lane-departure crashes (15 total, 1 KSI) make this corridor a strong fit.

psc.title: Road Diets
psc.tagline: A Road Diet, or roadway reconfiguration, can improve safety, calm traffic, provide better mobility and access for all road users, and enhance overall quality of life.
psc.narrative: A Road Diet typically involves converting an existing four-lane undivided roadway to a three-lane roadway consisting of two through lanes and a center two-way left-turn lane (TWLTL). This reconfiguration reduces rear-end and left-turn crashes due to the dedicated left-turn lane and decreases right-angle crashes as side street motorists cross fewer travel lanes.
psc.crash_reduction: 19-47% reduction in total crashes
psc.source_full: Federal Highway Administration — Proven Safety Countermeasure
psc.image_alt: Before and after example of a Road Diet.
psc.why_text: lane-departure crashes (15 total, 1 KSI) make this corridor a strong fit.

psc.title: Roundabouts
psc.tagline: The modern roundabout is an intersection with a circular configuration that safely and efficiently moves traffic and substantially reduces fatal and injury crashes.
psc.narrative: Roundabouts feature channelized, curved approaches that reduce vehicle speed, entry yield control that gives right-of-way to circulating traffic, and counterclockwise flow around a central island that minimizes conflict points. The net result of lower speeds and reduced conflicts is an environment where crashes that cause injury or fatality are substantially reduced.
psc.crash_reduction: 82% reduction in fatal and injury crashes
psc.source_full: Federal Highway Administration — Proven Safety Countermeasure
psc.image_alt: Example of a single-lane roundabout.
psc.why_text: intersection crashes (21 total, 2 KSI) make this corridor a strong fit.

They provide for deceleration prior to a turn and storage for vehicles waiting to complete a turn, reducing the potential for crashes . This countermeasure primarily reduces collisions of vehicles turning left across opposing through traffic and rear-end collisions with vehicles following closely behind .
psc.crash_reduction: 28-48% reduction in total crashes
psc.source_full: Federal Highway Administration — Proven Safety Countermeasure
psc.image_alt: Left- and right-turn lanes at a two-way stop-controlled intersection.
psc.why_text: intersection crashes (246 total, 22 KSI) make this corridor a strong fit.

psc.title: Leading Pedestrian Interval
psc.tagline: LPIs reduce potential conflicts between pedestrians and turning vehicles.
psc.narrative: A leading pedestrian interval (LPI) gives pedestrians the opportunity to enter the crosswalk at an intersection 3-7 seconds before vehicles are given a green indication. This allows pedestrians to better establish their presence in the crosswalk before vehicles have priority to turn right or left. By increasing visibility and reducing conflicts, this countermeasure can lead to a 13% reduction in pedestrian-vehicle crashes at intersections.
psc.crash_reduction: 13% reduction in pedestrian-vehicle crashes at intersections
psc.source_full: Federal Highway Administration — Proven Safety Countermeasure
psc.image_alt: An LPI allows a pedestrian to establish a presence in the crosswalk before vehicles are given a green indication.
psc.why_text: intersection crashes (246 total, 22 KSI) make this corridor a strong fit.

psc.title: Roundabouts
psc.tagline: The modern roundabout is an intersection with a circular configuration that safely and efficiently moves traffic and substantially reduces fatal and injury crashes.
psc.narrative: Roundabouts feature channelized, curved approaches that reduce vehicle speed, entry yield control that gives right-of-way to circulating traffic, and counterclockwise flow around a central island that minimizes conflict points. The net result of lower speeds and reduced conflicts is an environment where crashes that cause injury or fatality are substantially reduced.
psc.crash_reduction: 82% reduction in fatal and injury crashes
psc.source_full: Federal Highway Administration — Proven Safety Countermeasure
psc.image_alt: Example of a single-lane roundabout.

psc.title: Backplates with Retroreflective Borders
psc.tagline: Backplates with retroreflective borders improve the visibility of traffic signals to reduce crashes at signalized intersections.
psc.narrative: Backplates added to a traffic signal head improve the visibility of the illuminated face of the signal by introducing a controlled-contrast background. The improved visibility is made even more conspicuous by framing it with a 1- to 3-inch yellow retroreflective border, which is highly visible in both daytime and nighttime conditions. This treatment acts as a human factors enhancement that reduces total crashes at signalized intersections.
psc.crash_reduction: 15% reduction in total crashes
psc.source_full: Federal Highway Administration — Proven Safety Countermeasure
psc.image_alt: A conventional traffic signal with a signal backplate and yellow retroreflective border.

psc.title: Pedestrian Safety Islands
psc.tagline: A pedestrian safety island reduces the exposure time experienced by a pedestrian in the intersection.
psc.narrative: Pedestrian safety islands reduce exposure time in intersections by allowing pedestrians to cross one direction of traffic at a time. They are particularly effective on wide streets with high speeds or volumes, and should be at least 6 feet wide to accommodate bicycles or strollers. Design elements often include a 'nose' that extends past the crosswalk to protect waiting pedestrians and slow turning vehicles.

psc.title: Transit Streets

psc.title: Road Safety Audit
psc.tagline: Road Safety Audits (RSAs) provide an independent, multidisciplinary assessment of roadway safety to address limitations in traditional safety review procedures and reduce crashes.
psc.narrative: RSAs are performed by a multidisciplinary team independent of the project to consider all road users and account for human factors. They are documented in a formal report and require a formal response from the road owner, helping to reduce the number and severity of crashes due to safer designs.
psc.crash_reduction: 10-60% reduction in total crashes
psc.source_full: Federal Highway Administration — Proven Safety Countermeasure
psc.image_alt: Multi-disciplinary team performs field review during an RSA.

psc.title: Corridor Access Management
psc.tagline: Every intersection, from a signalized intersection to an unpaved driveway, has the potential for conflicts between vehicles, pedestrians, and bicyclists.
psc.narrative: Access management refers to the design, application, and control of entry and exit points along a roadway, including intersections and driveways. By managing the number and types of conflict points—locations where the travel paths of two users intersect—this countermeasure enhances safety and reduces total and fatal/injury crashes for all modes.
psc.crash_reduction: 5-23% reduction in total crashes along 2-lane rural roads and 25-31% reduction in fatal and injury crashes along urban/suburban arterials.
psc.source_full: Federal Highway Administration — Proven Safety Countermeasure

bca.sources: USDOT Departmental Guidance on Valuation of a Statistical Life (VSL $13.2M in 2023 dollars; serious injury = MAIS-3 fraction 0.105); FY2025 USDOT Benefit-Cost Analysis Guidance (values in 2023 dollars); 7% real discount rate per OMB Circular A-94 (1992 edition, reinstated April 2025, adopted in USDOT's May 2025 BCA guidance revision).

equity.method: length-weighted tract average (ACS 5-year); income is population-weighted mean of tract medians

hotspot.method: Corridor divided into fixed, non-overlapping 0.5-mile windows; crashes assigned by nearest-point projection onto the centerline. Simplified analog of the sliding-window hotspot method in Toole Design's Safer Streets Priority Finder (which slides overlapping windows across an entire network to discover corridors from scratch; here windows are fixed and applied only within this already-selected corridor).
